_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. IMDG / Annex III Documentation System**

Scope: All ocean shipments (FCL and LCL) containing goods that may be classified as marine pollutants under the IMDG Code. This is directly relevant for the workspace's humanitarian vertical (cleaning agents, chemicals), high-value automotive (battery shipments including sodium-ion batteries now under UN 3551/3552 per IMDG Amendment 42-24), and film/TV production (specialty chemicals, pressurised gases).

Requirement: The workspace must maintain or access a IMDG Code lookup system (updated to Amendment 42-24, operative 2024) enabling cargo classification against marine pollutant status. Internal booking forms must capture marine pollutant status at intake. DGD templates must be current and include all mandatory fields per Annex III and the IMDG Code. Container Packing Certificates must be obtained and filed for FCL dangerous goods movements.

Gap from baseline: Workspaces without a current IMDG Amendment 42-24 update to internal classification databases are at immediate risk; new UN numbers for sodium-ion batteries (UN 3551/3552) are already operative and frequently appear in luxury/automotive and live events (e-mobility support equipment) cargo streams.

Deadline: Immediate — IMDG Amendment 42-24 is operative now.

**2. ECA Route Monitoring and Carrier Compliance Tracking**

Scope: All ocean freight bookings on routes transiting Mediterranean, Baltic, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean, Norwegian Sea, and Canadian Arctic ECAs — covering the workspace's primary trade lanes (Americas/Europe/Asia).

Requirement: The workspace should implement a carrier compliance checklist embedded in ocean booking workflow, requiring each carrier/vessel to confirm:
- IAPP Certificate currency and sulphur compliance method (compliant fuel ≤0.10% m/m or EGCS)
- CII rating (for ships ≥5,000 GT) — directly affects operational efficiency and potential surcharges
- Tier III NOx engine certification for new vessels on Canadian Arctic and Norwegian Sea routes
- BDN availability if carrier uses low-flashpoint fuels (requirement in force 1 August 2025)

Gap from baseline: Most freight forwarders do not systematically collect IAPP Certificate or CII data at booking stage. The Mediterranean ECA compliance obligation (active since 1 May 2025) means this gap is already live for European trade lanes.

Deadline: Immediate for Mediterranean; before 1 March 2027 for Norwegian Sea/Canadian Arctic SOx/PM.

**3. Staff Training — IMDG Code and MARPOL Annex III**

Scope: All operational staff who intake, classify, document, or supervise ocean shipments.

Requirement: Shore-based personnel handling dangerous goods ocean shipments must hold current IMDG Code training, with training records available to competent authorities on request. The employer carries liability for ensuring training record availability. Training must cover Amendment 42-24 updates including new UN numbers and marine pollutant marking requirements.

Gap from baseline: Training refreshes tied to biennial IMDG amendment cycles are often overlooked; Amendment 42-24 is the current operative edition and training records should reflect it.

Deadline: Immediate for any staff processing ocean DG shipments; no later than 60 days for full team refresh.

**4. Supplier and Carrier Contract Clauses**

Scope: Ocean carrier agreements, NVOCC sub-contracting arrangements, and shipper booking conditions.

Requirement: Ocean carrier contracts should include representations that the carrier maintains a current IAPP Certificate, operates in ECA-compliance, and will notify the workspace of any vessel substitution that may introduce a non-compliant vessel. Where the workspace acts as NVOCC and issues own BLs, legal counsel should confirm liability exposure under applicable national MARPOL implementing legislation (e.g., US APPS). Shipper booking conditions should require shippers to declare marine pollutant status at time of booking and provide a completed DGD before cargo is accepted for ocean carriage.

Gap from baseline: Standard forwarder trading conditions frequently do not address MARPOL-specific representations. The expansion of ECAs and new GHG frameworks will generate increasing surcharge pass-through provisions that require explicit contract treatment.

Deadline: Next contract review cycle or within 90 days.

**5. Carbon/GHG Data Collection Infrastructure (Forward-Looking)**

Scope: Applicable when the workspace is required or chooses to report scope 3 ocean freight emissions.

Requirement: MEPC 83 approved amendments to IMO DCS (Reg. 27 of Annex VI) to improve accessibility of fuel oil consumption data. When these amendments enter force (~2027), the workspace may be able to extract voyage-level fuel consumption data from carriers via the IMO DCS to support its own GHG reporting. The workspace should begin mapping which carriers currently participate in the IMO DCS and whether voyage-level data is accessible through existing carrier portals.

Deadline: Begin assessment within 6 months; implement before GHG reporting requirements take effect.

*Source: LR Class News, Lloyd's Register, various 2024–2025. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/marpol-international-convention-for-the-prevention-of-pollution/. IMO MEPC/ES.2 Meeting Summary, IMO, October 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/meetingsummaries/pages/mepc-es-2.aspx. IMDG Code Guide, Gateway Cargo Ltd, March 2026. https://www.gateway-cargo.com/2026/03/25/imdg-code-a-comprehensive-guide-to-shipping-dangerous-goods-by-sea-in-2026/*
